items.q: What is the difference between invention and innovation?
items.correct: Invention is creating a genuinely new idea or device; innovation is successfully developing and commercialising it so the market adopts it.
items.misconception: They are the same thing - every invention is automatically an innovation.
items.random_wrong: Innovation only refers to changes in company logos.
items.why: Many inventions never reach the market. Innovation requires the extra steps of development, manufacture and successful diffusion to consumers.

items.q: Does market pull mean a company pushes its products onto the market?
items.correct: No - market pull means consumer demand pulls new products into existence; technology push is when new technology drives development before demand exists.
items.misconception: Yes - market pull is when firms push advertising at consumers.
items.random_wrong: Market pull means withdrawing products that fail.
items.why: The words invite reversal. Anchor them to the driver: pull starts with the consumer need; push starts with the new technology looking for an application.

items.q: Is a brand just a logo?
items.correct: No - a brand is the whole identity and perception of a product or company, including its values, reputation and customer experience; the logo is only one visible symbol.
items.misconception: Yes - the brand and the logo are the same thing.
items.random_wrong: A brand is the barcode printed on the packaging.
items.why: Logos are the most visible element, so students reduce brand to graphics. Brand value lives in consumer trust and associations, which a logo merely triggers.

items.q: Innovation differs from invention because innovation is:
items.correct: The successful commercial development and adoption of an idea in the market
items.misconception: Any sketch of a new product
items.random_wrong: The legal document protecting an idea
items.why: It's The successful commercial development and adoption of an idea in the market. “Any sketch of a new product” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A product developed in response to an identified consumer demand is an example of:
items.why: Correct answer: Market pull. “Technology push” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which scenario best illustrates technology push?
items.correct: A new material from a research lab inspires an entirely new product category
items.misconception: A survey reveals customers want quieter vacuum cleaners
items.random_wrong: A retailer requests a cheaper version of an existing toy
items.why: It's A new material from a research lab inspires an entirely new product category. “A survey reveals customers want quieter vacuum cleaners” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Dividing consumers into groups with shared characteristics to target products at them
items.misconception: Selling one identical product to everyone
items.why: It's Dividing consumers into groups with shared characteristics to target products at them. “Selling one identical product to everyone” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A brand sells 30,000 units in a market where 120,000 units are sold in total. What is its market share?
items.why: Exact answer: 25 %. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
